name: Bharata
description: Bharata is a prominent figure in Hindu mythology, particularly revered for his unwavering devotion to his elder brother Rama and his exemplary rule as a regent. He is a symbol of righteousness, selflessness, and brotherly love. While Rama was exiled, Bharata refused to assume the throne, instead placing Rama's sandals on the throne as a symbol of Rama's authority and ruling as his representative. His reign was characterized by justice, prosperity, and adherence to Dharma. Bharata's story is a testament to the importance of duty, integrity, and the power of familial bonds in Hindu culture. He embodies the ideal of a virtuous ruler, prioritizing the well-being of his people and upholding Dharma above personal ambition. He is considered an important example of dharma and selfless dedication, especially in the context of leadership and kinship. His story is a central part of the Ramayana epic, shaping cultural understandings of governance and familial loyalty.
subtitle: The Righteous Regent

keyMyths.title: Bharata's Refusal of the Throne
keyMyths.description: When Rama was exiled by Kaikeyi, Bharata was summoned back to Ayodhya, expecting to be crowned king. However, upon learning of the injustice done to Rama, Bharata vehemently refused to accept the throne. He rebuked his mother Kaikeyi for her actions and embarked on a journey to find Rama and persuade him to return to Ayodhya. This act of selflessness and devotion cemented Bharata's reputation as a paragon of virtue.

keyMyths.title: Bharata's Regency
keyMyths.description: When Bharata failed to convince Rama to return immediately, he obtained Rama's sandals (padukas) and placed them on the throne as a symbol of Rama's rule. He then governed Ayodhya as Rama's regent, living an ascetic life and dedicating himself to the welfare of his people. His reign was marked by justice, prosperity, and adherence to Dharma, setting a standard for righteous governance.

keyMyths.title: Bharata Meets Hanuman
keyMyths.description: While Bharata was waiting for Rama's return at the end of the exile, he nearly killed Hanuman, mistaking him for a messenger bringing bad news. Hanuman was flying to Ayodhya to inform Bharata of Rama's imminent return. This incident highlights Bharata's fierce loyalty and protectiveness towards Rama, showing his willingness to defend Rama's honor and the throne, even against a powerful figure like Hanuman.
